input_text: Washington (CNN) -- The Supreme Court on Monday declined to hear a challenge to a federal law that makes it a crime to encourage or induce someone to enter the United States illegally. The justices, without comment, let stand a lower court ruling that found the law constitutional. The case was brought by Evelyn Sineneng-Smith, an immigration consultant from San Jose, California, who was convicted of encouraging two foreign nationals to remain in the United States illegally for her own financial gain. She charged them fees for filing labor certification applications, even though she knew they were ineligible for the visas. Her lawyers argued that the statute was unconstitutionally overbroad because it could criminalize everyday speech such as telling someone "I encourage you to stay in the United States." The 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals rejected that argument, finding the law applies only to conduct that facilitates illegal immigration, not protected speech. Legal experts said the decision leaves intact a powerful tool for prosecutors to go after those who profit from illegal immigration schemes.
gold_summary: The Supreme Court declined to hear a challenge to a law criminalizing encouragement of illegal immigration. Immigration consultant Evelyn Sineneng-Smith had argued the law was overbroad, but the 9th Circuit found it applies only to conduct facilitating illegal immigration, not protected speech.

input_text: London (CNN) -- British police have arrested a 25-year-old man in connection with an alleged plot to attack military personnel, Scotland Yard said Wednesday. The suspect was detained in a dawn raid at a residential address in east London on suspicion of being concerned in the commission, preparation, or instigation of acts of terrorism, the Metropolitan Police said. Officers from the Met's Counter Terrorism Command are leading the investigation. A search is being carried out at the address where the arrest was made. The arrest was intelligence-led and pre-planned, police said. The suspect remains in custody at a south London police station. The investigation is ongoing and police have not released further details about the nature of the alleged plot. Authorities said there was no immediate threat to the public. The arrest comes amid heightened security concerns across Europe following a series of attacks inspired by extremist groups.
gold_summary: British police arrested a 25-year-old man in east London in connection with a suspected plot against military personnel. The intelligence-led arrest was made by the Counter Terrorism Command, and authorities said there was no immediate public threat.

input_text: New York (CNN) -- A massive winter storm brought blizzard conditions to the northeastern United States on Tuesday, dumping up to 30 inches of snow in some areas and leaving hundreds of thousands without power. The storm, which forecasters called a "bomb cyclone" due to its rapid intensification, battered the coast from Virginia to Maine with hurricane-force wind gusts exceeding 70 mph. More than 4,500 flights were canceled across the country, with major airports in New York, Boston, and Philadelphia suspending operations. Transportation officials urged residents to stay off the roads as visibility dropped to near zero in many areas. At least seven deaths were attributed to the storm, including several from hypothermia and traffic accidents. Power outages affected more than 350,000 customers in Massachusetts alone, with utility crews working around the clock to restore service. Governor Charlie Baker declared a state of emergency and activated the National Guard to assist with rescue operations. Forecasters said the storm would continue through Wednesday morning before tapering off, with temperatures expected to plummet into the single digits behind the system.
gold_summary: A massive 'bomb cyclone' winter storm hit the northeastern U.S., dumping up to 30 inches of snow with hurricane-force winds. Over 4,500 flights were canceled, 350,000+ lost power in Massachusetts, and at least seven deaths were reported. Governor Baker declared a state of emergency.

input_text: San Francisco (CNN) -- Tech giant Google announced Tuesday that it will invest $10 billion over the next five years to expand its operations across the United States, creating thousands of jobs in data centers and office facilities. The investment will focus on 11 states, including Texas, Virginia, Nebraska, and Nevada, where the company plans to build new data centers to support its growing cloud computing business. CEO Sundar Pichai said the expansion reflects Google's commitment to being a "helpful company" beyond its California headquarters. The announcement comes as major tech companies face increasing scrutiny over their dominance and economic influence. Google's parent company Alphabet reported revenue of $75 billion in the most recent quarter, driven largely by advertising and cloud services. The company currently employs about 190,000 people worldwide. Local officials in the targeted states welcomed the investment, noting it would bring high-paying jobs and tax revenue to their communities. In Nevada, the governor said the new data center would create approximately 1,000 construction jobs and 100 permanent positions.
gold_summary: Google announced a $10 billion investment plan over five years to expand U.S. operations across 11 states, focusing on data centers and office facilities. CEO Sundar Pichai emphasized the company's commitment beyond California, while local officials welcomed the jobs and tax revenue the expansion would bring.

input_text: Beijing (CNN) -- China's economy grew at its slowest pace in nearly three decades last year, expanding just 6.1% as the country grappled with the effects of an ongoing trade war with the United States and weakening global demand. The figure, released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Friday, was within the government's target range of 6% to 6.5% but marked a significant deceleration from the 6.6% growth recorded in 2018. The slowdown was felt most acutely in the manufacturing sector, where factory output fell to its lowest level in 17 years. Consumer spending remained relatively robust, contributing about 57% of GDP growth, according to government data. Officials pointed to signs of stabilization in the fourth quarter, when growth held steady at 6% compared to the previous quarter. Premier Li Keqiang acknowledged the challenges facing the economy but expressed confidence that China would meet its growth targets. Economists said the Phase One trade deal signed with the United States in January could provide some relief, but warned that structural challenges including high debt levels and an aging population would continue to weigh on growth.
gold_summary: China's economy grew 6.1% last year, its slowest pace in nearly three decades, amid the U.S. trade war and weak global demand. Manufacturing hit a 17-year low, though consumer spending remained strong. Economists said the Phase One trade deal may help, but structural challenges persist.

input_text: Paris (CNN) -- France announced a sweeping ban on single-use plastics starting in 2025, becoming one of the most aggressive countries in Europe to tackle plastic pollution. The new law, passed by the National Assembly on Thursday, will prohibit plastic packaging for most fruits and vegetables, ban plastic toys in children's meals at fast-food restaurants, and require businesses to provide water fountains instead of plastic water bottles. Environmental Minister Barbara Pompili called the legislation "a decisive step" in France's fight against plastic waste, noting that the country currently produces 4.5 million tons of plastic waste annually, of which only 26% is recycled. Critics from the packaging industry argued that the timeline is too aggressive and could increase costs for consumers and small businesses. The law also includes measures to promote reusable containers, with a target of having 20% of retail floor space dedicated to package-free products by 2030. France already banned single-use plastic bags in 2016 and plastic straws in 2021.
gold_summary: France passed a sweeping law banning single-use plastics starting in 2025, targeting plastic packaging for produce and plastic toys in fast-food meals. The country produces 4.5 million tons of plastic waste yearly with only 26% recycled, and the new law aims to promote reusable containers and package-free retail.

input_text: Houston (CNN) -- NASA astronauts successfully completed a critical spacewalk on Wednesday to repair a malfunctioning cooling system on the International Space Station, averting what officials called a potentially serious situation. The six-hour spacewalk, conducted by astronauts Jessica Meir and Christina Koch, involved replacing a failed ammonia pump module on the station's truss structure. It was the third all-female spacewalk in history. The cooling system failure was detected last week when sensors showed a drop in pressure in one of the station's two external cooling loops. Without the repair, the station would have had to shut down several science experiments and reduce power to non-essential systems. Flight controllers in Houston guided the astronauts through the complex procedure, which required disconnecting and reconnecting multiple fluid lines while handling toxic ammonia. Both astronauts are veteran spacewalkers with extensive training on station maintenance procedures. NASA Administrator Bill Nelson praised the crew's performance, calling it "a testament to the skill and dedication of our astronaut corps." The station, which has been continuously inhabited since 2000, orbits Earth at approximately 17,500 mph.
gold_summary: NASA astronauts Jessica Meir and Christina Koch completed a six-hour spacewalk to repair a malfunctioning cooling system on the International Space Station. The third all-female spacewalk involved replacing a failed ammonia pump module, averting the need to shut down experiments and reduce power.

input_text: Nairobi (CNN) -- A devastating locust swarm, the worst in 25 years, has descended on East Africa, threatening food security for millions of people across Kenya, Ethiopia, and Somalia. The swarms, some covering areas of up to 2,400 square kilometers, can consume the same amount of food in a day as 35,000 people, according to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization. Kenya has deployed aircraft to spray pesticides on the swarms, but experts say the effort has been hampered by a lack of equipment and the sheer scale of the infestation. The locusts, which breed in moist sandy soil, have thrived due to unusually heavy rainfall linked to a positive Indian Ocean Dipole — a climate pattern that brings wet conditions to East Africa. The FAO has called for $76 million in international aid to combat the crisis, warning that if left unchecked, the locust population could grow 500-fold by June. The World Food Programme estimates that 12 million people in the region already face food insecurity, and the locusts could push millions more into crisis. Small-scale farmers, who make up the majority of the agricultural sector in the affected countries, are particularly vulnerable.
gold_summary: The worst locust swarm in 25 years is devastating East Africa, with swarms consuming as much food daily as 35,000 people across Kenya, Ethiopia, and Somalia. The FAO has requested $76 million in aid, warning the population could grow 500-fold by June, threatening 12 million already food-insecure people.

input_text: Tokyo (CNN) -- Japan's government approved a record defense budget of $52 billion on Friday, marking a significant shift in the country's security posture amid growing tensions in the Asia-Pacific region. The budget, a 7.3% increase from the previous year, includes funding for long-range cruise missiles, advanced fighter jets, and expanded cyber warfare capabilities. Prime Minister Fumio Kishida said the spending increase was necessary to counter threats from China's military buildup, North Korea's missile program, and Russia's aggression in Ukraine. The decision represents a dramatic departure from Japan's post-World War II pacifist stance, which had traditionally kept defense spending at about 1% of GDP. Under the new plan, Japan aims to reach 2% of GDP in defense spending within five years, bringing it in line with NATO standards. Opposition lawmakers criticized the budget increase, arguing it could escalate regional tensions and strain government finances at a time when the country is already dealing with a shrinking population and mounting debt. The United States, Japan's primary security ally, welcomed the move.
gold_summary: Japan approved a record $52 billion defense budget, a 7.3% increase, to counter threats from China, North Korea, and Russia. The move marks a departure from Japan's pacifist stance, aiming to reach 2% of GDP in defense spending within five years, in line with NATO standards.

input_text: Los Angeles (CNN) -- California's largest utility, Pacific Gas & Electric, began shutting off power to nearly 1 million customers on Wednesday as extreme wind conditions heightened the risk of catastrophic wildfires across the state. The planned outage, known as a Public Safety Power Shutoff, affected residents in 36 counties from the Sierra Nevada foothills to the wine country north of San Francisco. PG&E said the shutoffs were necessary because strong winds — with gusts expected to exceed 65 mph in some areas — could damage power lines and spark fires in dry vegetation. The utility faces billions of dollars in liability from previous wildfires linked to its equipment, including the devastating Camp Fire in 2018 that killed 85 people and destroyed the town of Paradise. Governor Gavin Newsom criticized PG&E's handling of the shutoffs, saying the company's years of mismanagement led to the need for such drastic measures. Hospitals and emergency facilities were exempt from the shutoffs and switched to backup generators. PG&E said it would take several days to restore power after the wind event passes, as crews would need to inspect thousands of miles of power lines for damage before re-energizing them.
gold_summary: PG&E shut off power to nearly 1 million California customers across 36 counties due to extreme wind conditions and wildfire risk. The utility, facing billions in liability from past fires including the deadly 2018 Camp Fire, said the shutoffs were necessary as gusts could exceed 65 mph. Governor Newsom criticized PG&E's mismanagement.

input_text: Geneva (CNN) -- The World Health Organization declared the novel coronavirus outbreak a global health emergency on Thursday, as the number of confirmed cases worldwide surpassed 8,000. WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus praised China's response to the outbreak but said the declaration was made due to concern about the virus spreading to countries with weaker health systems. The virus, which originated in a seafood market in Wuhan, China, has killed 170 people and spread to 18 countries. The emergency declaration, formally known as a Public Health Emergency of International Concern, allows the WHO to coordinate the international response and issue recommendations to member states. Several countries, including the United States, Japan, and France, have begun evacuating their citizens from Wuhan and surrounding areas. Airlines including British Airways and Lufthansa have suspended flights to mainland China. The WHO did not recommend trade or travel restrictions, saying such measures could increase fear and stigma without providing additional public health benefit.
gold_summary: The WHO declared the coronavirus outbreak a global health emergency as cases surpassed 8,000 with 170 deaths across 18 countries. Director-General Tedros cited concerns about spread to countries with weaker health systems. Several nations began evacuations from Wuhan while major airlines suspended China flights.

input_text: New Delhi (CNN) -- India's Parliament passed a controversial citizenship law on Wednesday that fast-tracks naturalization for religious minorities from three neighboring countries — but excludes Muslims. The Citizenship Amendment Act grants citizenship to Hindus, Buddhists, Christians, Sikhs, Jains, and Parsis who fled persecution in Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan before 2015. Critics say the law, combined with a proposed nationwide citizenship registry, could be used to marginalize India's 200 million Muslims. Prime Minister Narendra Modi defended the legislation, saying it was designed to protect persecuted minorities and did not affect any Indian citizen regardless of religion. Protests erupted across the country, with demonstrators clashing with police in several states. In Assam, where residents fear the law will encourage migration from Bangladesh, authorities imposed a curfew and suspended internet services. Opposition leaders called the law unconstitutional and said it violates India's secular principles. The United Nations human rights office called the law "fundamentally discriminatory," while the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom urged sanctions against Indian officials.
gold_summary: India's Parliament passed the Citizenship Amendment Act, which fast-tracks citizenship for religious minorities from neighboring countries but excludes Muslims. The law sparked nationwide protests, with critics calling it discriminatory and unconstitutional. The UN called it 'fundamentally discriminatory' while PM Modi defended it as protecting persecuted minorities.

input_text: Miami (CNN) -- A 12-story condominium building partially collapsed in the middle of the night Thursday in Surfside, Florida, killing at least one person and leaving 99 unaccounted for in one of the deadliest building failures in U.S. history. Rescue crews worked through the rubble searching for survivors as family members gathered at a nearby community center, desperate for news of loved ones. The collapse occurred around 1:30 a.m. at Champlain Towers South, a 40-year-old oceanfront building. Roughly 55 of the building's 136 units were destroyed. Video from the scene showed a large section of the building reduced to a pile of concrete and steel, while the remaining portion stood precariously. Governor Ron DeSantis declared a state of emergency and requested federal assistance. Engineers said the cause of the collapse would take months to determine, but preliminary reports pointed to possible structural degradation of concrete columns in the building's underground parking garage. Residents of the remaining portion of the building were evacuated.
gold_summary: A 12-story condominium in Surfside, Florida partially collapsed overnight, killing at least one person with 99 unaccounted for. About 55 of 136 units in the 40-year-old Champlain Towers South were destroyed. Governor DeSantis declared a state of emergency as engineers investigated possible structural degradation.

input_text: Brussels (CNN) -- The European Union reached a landmark agreement early Tuesday to regulate artificial intelligence, becoming the first major governmental body to establish comprehensive rules for AI systems. The AI Act, which was negotiated over three years, classifies AI applications by risk level and imposes strict requirements on high-risk uses such as facial recognition, hiring algorithms, and critical infrastructure management. Real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces will be banned except in cases involving serious criminal threats. Companies that violate the rules could face fines of up to 7% of their global annual revenue. The legislation also requires AI-generated content, including deepfakes, to be clearly labeled. Tech industry representatives expressed mixed reactions, with some praising the clarity the rules provide and others warning that strict regulation could stifle innovation and push AI development outside Europe. The law is expected to take effect in 2025, with a phased implementation period. U.S. and Chinese officials are closely watching the EU's approach as they develop their own AI governance frameworks.
gold_summary: The EU reached a landmark agreement on the AI Act, the first comprehensive AI regulation by a major government. The law classifies AI by risk level, bans most real-time biometric surveillance, and imposes fines up to 7% of global revenue for violations. It takes effect in 2025.

input_text: Sydney (CNN) -- Australia experienced its hottest day on record Wednesday, with the national average temperature reaching 41.9 degrees Celsius (107.4 degrees Fahrenheit), shattering the previous record set just the day before. The extreme heat has fueled devastating bushfires across New South Wales and Victoria, with more than 200 fires burning simultaneously. Firefighters said conditions were "catastrophic" in several regions, with strong winds pushing flames toward populated areas. Tens of thousands of residents have been forced to evacuate coastal towns, and the smoke from the fires has created hazardous air quality conditions in Sydney and Melbourne. At least 28 people have died in the fire season, which began earlier and more intensely than usual. More than 15 million acres have burned — an area larger than Denmark. Prime Minister Scott Morrison faced criticism for taking an overseas vacation during the crisis and for his government's climate change policies. Climate scientists said the extreme conditions were consistent with long-term warming trends and warned that such events would become more frequent and intense without significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions.
gold_summary: Australia recorded its hottest day ever at 41.9°C as over 200 bushfires tore through New South Wales and Victoria. At least 28 people died and 15 million acres burned in a fire season that started earlier and more intensely than usual. PM Morrison faced criticism over his response and climate policies.

input_text: Stockholm (CNN) -- Swedish climate activist Greta Thunberg was named Time magazine's Person of the Year for 2019, becoming the youngest individual ever to receive the distinction. The 16-year-old, who began her activism by skipping school every Friday to protest outside the Swedish Parliament, has inspired millions of young people worldwide to demand urgent action on climate change. Time editor-in-chief Edward Felsenthal said Thunberg was chosen because she had become "the biggest voice on the biggest issue facing the planet." Thunberg's "Fridays for Future" movement has grown into a global phenomenon, with climate strikes held in more than 150 countries. She addressed the United Nations Climate Action Summit in September, delivering an impassioned speech in which she told world leaders: "How dare you?" The selection drew both praise and criticism. Supporters called her an inspiration, while detractors questioned whether a teenager could fully understand the complex economic trade-offs involved in climate policy.
gold_summary: Greta Thunberg, 16, was named Time's Person of the Year 2019, the youngest ever. Her 'Fridays for Future' movement sparked climate strikes in over 150 countries after she began protesting outside Sweden's Parliament. She gained global attention with her 'How dare you?' speech at the UN Climate Summit.

input_text: Moscow (CNN) -- Russia successfully tested a hypersonic missile system on Wednesday that President Vladimir Putin described as "invincible" and capable of evading any existing missile defense system. The Avangard hypersonic glide vehicle traveled at Mach 27 — roughly 20,000 mph — during the test launch from the Dombarovsky missile base in the Ural Mountains. It hit a target on the Kura test range in Kamchatka, approximately 3,700 miles away. Putin watched the launch via video link from the National Defense Control Center in Moscow and declared the test a complete success. The weapon system is designed to sit atop an intercontinental ballistic missile and maneuver unpredictably at extreme speeds, making it nearly impossible to intercept with current technology. U.S. defense officials have expressed concern about Russia's hypersonic weapons program, with the Pentagon accelerating its own development efforts. Arms control experts warned that the deployment of hypersonic weapons could destabilize the nuclear balance by rendering existing defense systems obsolete and reducing decision-making time in a crisis.
gold_summary: Russia successfully tested the Avangard hypersonic glide vehicle, which traveled at Mach 27 across 3,700 miles. Putin called the weapon 'invincible' as it can evade existing missile defenses by maneuvering unpredictably at extreme speeds. Arms control experts warned it could destabilize the nuclear balance.

input_text: Cape Town (CNN) -- South Africa's Cape Town came within weeks of becoming the first major city in the world to run out of water before emergency conservation measures and the arrival of winter rains averted the crisis. The city of four million people had been counting down to "Day Zero" — the date when municipal water supplies would be cut off and residents would have to queue at distribution points for their daily water ration. At the peak of the crisis, residents were limited to 50 liters (13 gallons) of water per day, roughly one-sixth of the average American's daily usage. The crisis was caused by three consecutive years of below-average rainfall combined with rapid population growth and inadequate infrastructure investment. Mayor Dan Plato credited residents' conservation efforts with postponing Day Zero indefinitely, noting that water consumption dropped by more than 50% as people took shorter showers, recycled gray water, and stopped watering gardens. The city is now investing in desalination plants, groundwater extraction, and water recycling facilities to diversify its water supply and prevent a future crisis.
gold_summary: Cape Town nearly became the first major city to run dry, facing a 'Day Zero' cutoff after three years of drought. Residents were limited to 50 liters daily and cut consumption by over 50%, averting the crisis. The city is now investing in desalination and water recycling infrastructure.

input_text: Singapore (CNN) -- A major ransomware attack has crippled operations at one of the world's largest shipping companies, disrupting global supply chains and causing delays at ports across Asia and Europe. The attack, which began over the weekend, forced the company to shut down its IT systems and revert to manual operations at terminals in Singapore, Rotterdam, and Los Angeles. Cybersecurity experts said the malware appeared to be a variant of the LockBit ransomware, which encrypts files and demands payment in cryptocurrency for their release. The company has not confirmed whether a ransom was demanded or paid. Industry analysts estimated the attack could cost the company between $200 million and $300 million in lost revenue and recovery expenses. The incident highlights the vulnerability of global supply chains to cyberattacks, with shipping companies increasingly reliant on digital systems for cargo tracking, scheduling, and customs clearance. Government cybersecurity agencies in multiple countries are assisting with the investigation.
gold_summary: A major ransomware attack shut down IT systems at one of the world's largest shipping companies, disrupting ports in Singapore, Rotterdam, and Los Angeles. The LockBit variant malware could cost $200-300 million in damages. The incident underscores global supply chains' vulnerability to cyberattacks.

input_text: Mexico City (CNN) -- Mexico's president announced a groundbreaking agreement with the United States on Monday to address the root causes of migration from Central America through a joint $4 billion development program. The plan focuses on economic development in Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador — the three countries that produce the most migrants heading to the U.S. southern border. Funding will be directed toward job creation, agricultural modernization, violence reduction programs, and anti-corruption efforts. The agreement comes after months of negotiations between the two governments and follows a surge in migration that saw a record 2.4 million encounters at the U.S.-Mexico border last year. President López Obrador said the program would help people find opportunities in their home countries rather than making the dangerous journey north. U.S. officials said the plan would complement stricter border enforcement measures. Human rights organizations cautiously welcomed the development-focused approach but warned that previous aid programs had failed to significantly reduce migration due to corruption and lack of accountability in recipient countries.
gold_summary: Mexico and the U.S. announced a joint $4 billion program to address migration root causes in Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador through economic development and violence reduction. The agreement follows a record 2.4 million border encounters last year. Human rights groups cautiously welcomed the approach but warned of past aid failures.

import re
from collections import Counter
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple

def analyze_text(text: str) -> Dict[str, any]:
    """Analyze text and return statistics."""
    words = re.findall(r'\b[a-zA-Z]+\b', text.lower())
    sentences = re.split(r'[.!?]+', text)
    sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if s.strip()]
    
    word_freq = Counter(words)
    
    avg_word_length = sum(len(w) for w in words) / max(len(words), 1)
    avg_sentence_length = len(words) / max(len(sentences), 1)
    
    return {
        'total_words': len(words),
        'unique_words': len(set(words)),
        'total_sentences': len(sentences),
        'avg_word_length': round(avg_word_length, 2),
        'avg_sentence_length': round(avg_sentence_length, 2),
        'top_10_words': word_freq.most_common(10),
        'vocabulary_richness': round(len(set(words)) / max(len(words), 1), 4)
    }
gold_summary: A text analysis utility that computes various linguistic statistics from input text. It uses regex to tokenize words and split sentences, then calculates metrics including word count, unique words, average word/sentence length, top-10 word frequencies, and vocabulary richness ratio.

Tasks are ordered by priority number, supporting add, get-next (which skips completed tasks), peek, and pending count operations. The Task dataclass uses field ordering to ensure only priority is compared during heap operations.

An async-compatible LRU cache with optional TTL (time-to-live) expiration. Uses asyncio.Lock for thread safety, supports configurable max size with LRU eviction, and provides a decorator (cached) for transparently caching async function results based on argument hashing.

A configurable debounce utility function that delays function execution until after a specified wait period. Supports leading edge invocation, trailing edge invocation, and a maximum wait time to guarantee execution. Includes cancel and flush methods for manual control.

A generic doubly-linked list implementation in Python with type hints. Supports append, prepend, and O(1) node removal operations using prev/next pointers. Uses __slots__ on nodes for memory efficiency, and implements __iter__ and __reversed__ for bidirectional traversal.

A simplified blockchain implementation with proof-of-work mining. The Block class computes SHA-256 hashes from transaction data, while Blockchain manages a chain with genesis block creation, transaction queuing, nonce-based mining with configurable difficulty, and chain validation by verifying hash linkage.

A lightweight JavaScript HTTP router supporting GET, POST, PUT, DELETE methods with Express-style path parameters (e.g., :id). Features middleware chaining, regex-based URL pattern matching with named capture groups, and sequential handler execution with early-return support when a response is sent.

import sqlite3
from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Tuple

class SimpleORM:
    """A minimal ORM wrapper around SQLite."""
    
    def __init__(self, db_path: str = ':memory:'):
        self.db_path = db_path
        self._conn: Optional[sqlite3.Connection] = None
    
    @contextmanager
    def connection(self):
        conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)
        conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
        try:
            yield conn
            conn.commit()
        except Exception:
            conn.rollback()
            raise
        finally:
            conn.close()
    
    def create_table(self, name: str, columns: Dict[str, str]) -> None:
        cols = ', '.join(f'{col} {dtype}' for col, dtype in columns.items())
        with self.connection() as conn:
            conn.execute(f'CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS {name} ({cols})')
    
    def insert(self, table: str, data: Dict[str, Any]) -> int:
        cols = ', '.join(data.keys())
        placeholders = ', '.join('?' * len(data))
        with self.connection() as conn:
            cursor = conn.execute(
                f'INSERT INTO {table} ({cols}) VALUES ({placeholders})',
                list(data.values())
            )
            return cursor.lastrowid
    
    def select(self, table: str, where: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
               order_by: Optional[str] = None, limit: Optional[int] = None
    ) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
        query = f'SELECT * FROM {table}'
        params: list = []
        if where:
            conditions = ' AND '.join(f'{k} = ?' for k in where)
            query += f' WHERE {conditions}'
            params = list(where.values())
        if order_by:
            query += f' ORDER BY {order_by}'
        if limit:
            query += f' LIMIT {limit}'
        with self.connection() as conn:
            rows = conn.execute(query, params).fetchall()
            return [dict(row) for row in rows]
    
    def delete(self, table: str, where: Dict[str, Any]) -> int:
        conditions = ' AND '.join(f'{k} = ?' for k in where)
        with self.connection() as conn:
            cursor = conn.execute(
                f'DELETE FROM {table} WHERE {conditions}',
                list(where.values())
            )
            return cursor.rowcount
gold_summary: A minimal SQLite ORM providing CRUD operations with context-managed connections that auto-commit on success and rollback on error. Supports table creation, parameterized inserts, filtered selects with ordering/limiting, and conditional deletes. Uses sqlite3.Row for dictionary-style row access.

A simple Markdown-to-HTML parser that handles block elements (headings, lists, blockquotes, paragraphs) and inline formatting (bold, italic, code, links, images). Uses regex-based rules for inline transformations and line-by-line processing for block structure, with list aggregation for consecutive bullet points.

A minimal Virtual DOM implementation in JavaScript with createElement, render, and diff functions. createElement builds a virtual node tree, render converts it to real DOM elements with event listener support, and diff performs recursive reconciliation by comparing old and new virtual trees to make minimal DOM updates.

A from-scratch neural network implementation in Python without external libraries. Includes a NeuralLayer with He-initialized weights, forward pass computation, ReLU/sigmoid activations, and MSE loss. The SimpleNN class chains layers with ReLU for hidden layers and sigmoid for output, supporting feedforward prediction.

A disk-based key-value cache with TTL expiration and optional gzip compression. Stores JSON-serialized entries as files with configurable cache directory and default TTL. Supports get (with auto-expiry cleanup), set, delete, clear, and key listing operations, sanitizing keys for filesystem safety.

A finite state machine implementation in JavaScript with guard conditions, transition actions, and entry/exit hooks. Configured declaratively with states and events, supporting string or object transitions (with target, action, guard). Includes an event listener system for observing state transitions.

Tool Call: write_file(path='.github/workflows/ci.yml', content='name: CI/CD Pipeline
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.11"}
      - run: pip install ruff
      - run: ruff check src/ tests/
  test:
    needs: lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      - run: pytest --cov=src --cov-report=xml
      - uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
  build:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == "refs/heads/main"
    steps:
      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with: {push: true, tags: "ghcr.io/${{github.repository}}:${{github.sha}}"}

Agent Task: Investigate and fix a memory leak in a Node.js application that's causing OOM kills in production.

[Turn 1] Agent: Let me start by examining the memory usage patterns.
Tool Call: get_metrics(service='api-server', metric='memory_usage', period='7d')
Result: Memory steadily increasing from 256MB to 1.8GB over 7 days. OOM kills at 2GB limit: 3 in last 7 days. Restart temporarily fixes (drops to 256MB).

[Turn 2] Agent: Classic memory leak pattern. Let me take a heap snapshot.
Tool Call: capture_heap_snapshot(service='api-server', pod='api-server-7d8f9-xk2m4')
Result: Heap snapshot captured (1.4GB). Top retainers: EventEmitter listeners (450MB, 12,847 listeners), WeakRef cleanup callbacks (320MB), String (280MB), Closure (190MB), Array (160MB).

[Turn 3] Agent: 12,847 EventEmitter listeners is extremely suspicious — this is almost certainly an event listener leak. Let me find where listeners are being added without removal.
Tool Call: search_codebase(pattern='addEventListener|.on(|.addListener(', file_types=['*.js', '*.ts'])
Result: 47 matches. Suspicious patterns:
- src/websocket/handler.ts:34 — `socket.on('message', ...)` inside a loop without cleanup
- src/services/cache.ts:89 — `redis.on('error', ...)` registered every time `getConnection()` is called
- src/middleware/sse.ts:12 — `req.on('close', ...)` with closure capturing large response objects

[Turn 4] Agent: Found three leak sources. The redis connection is the worst — let me verify.
Tool Call: read_file(path='src/services/cache.ts')
Result: getConnection() creates a new Redis client on every call and adds error/reconnect listeners, but never closes old connections or removes listeners. Called ~2000 times/day.

Agent Fix:
Tool Call: write_file(path='src/services/cache.ts', content='[Refactored to use singleton Redis connection with lazy initialization, proper error handling, and connection pooling]')
Tool Call: write_file(path='src/websocket/handler.ts', content='[Added listener cleanup in disconnect handler, moved listener registration outside loop]')
Tool Call: write_file(path='src/middleware/sse.ts', content='[Nullified response object reference in close handler to allow GC]')

[Turn 5] Agent: Deployed fix to staging. Monitoring results after 24 hours:
Tool Call: get_metrics(service='api-server-staging', metric='memory_usage', period='24h')
Result: Memory stable at 280-310MB over 24 hours. No growth pattern detected. EventEmitter listeners steady at 45.

Agent: Memory leak is fixed. Root cause was three-fold: Redis connection leak (primary, ~60% of leak), WebSocket listener leak (~25%), and SSE closure retention (~15%).
gold_summary: An agent diagnosed and fixed a Node.js memory leak causing OOM kills at 2GB. Heap analysis revealed 12,847 leaked EventEmitter listeners (450MB). Three sources were identified: a Redis client creating new connections on every call (~60% of leak), WebSocket listeners added in a loop without cleanup (~25%), and SSE closures retaining large objects (~15%). Fixes included a singleton Redis connection, proper listener cleanup, and nullifying closure references. Post-fix memory stabilized at 280-310MB.

Agent Task: Optimize the performance of a slow PostgreSQL database query.

[Turn 1] Agent: Let me analyze the slow query.
User: This query takes 45 seconds: SELECT o.*, u.name, u.email FROM orders o JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id WHERE o.status = 'pending' AND o.created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50;

Tool Call: explain_analyze(query="SELECT o.*, u.name, u.email FROM orders o JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id WHERE o.status = 'pending' AND o.created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50")
Result: Execution plan:
- Seq Scan on orders: 12.4M rows scanned, Filter removes 99.7%
- Hash Join with users: 38,000 rows matched
- Sort: 38,000 rows sorted
- Actual time: 44,872ms
- Buffers: shared hit=89234 read=234567 (heavy disk I/O)

[Turn 2] Agent: The problem is clear: sequential scan on 12.4M rows with no index. Let me check existing indexes.
Tool Call: get_table_info(tables=['orders', 'users'])
Result: 
- orders: 12.4M rows, 28 GB. Indexes: PRIMARY (id), idx_user_id (user_id). No index on status or created_at.
- users: 2.1M rows, 4.2 GB. Indexes: PRIMARY (id), idx_email (email).
- orders.status distribution: 'pending' 0.3%, 'completed' 94.2%, 'cancelled' 5.5%

[Turn 3] Agent: Perfect storm of issues. Let me apply optimizations.

Tool Call: create_index(table='orders', columns=['status', 'created_at DESC'], name='idx_orders_status_created', where="status = 'pending'")
Result: Partial index created. Size: 12 MB (vs full index would be 890 MB).

Tool Call: explain_analyze(query="[same query]")
Result: New execution plan:
- Index Scan using idx_orders_status_created: 847 rows
- Nested Loop with users: 847 rows
- Actual time: 2.3ms
- Buffers: shared hit=127

[Turn 4] Agent: Query optimized from 44,872ms → 2.3ms (19,500x faster).

Key optimization: Created a **partial index** on (status, created_at DESC) WHERE status='pending'. Since only 0.3% of orders are pending, the partial index is 12 MB instead of 890 MB for a full index, while delivering the same performance benefit.

Additional recommendations:
1. Consider partitioning orders table by created_at (monthly)
2. Add covering index including user_id to avoid table lookups
3. Archive completed orders older than 1 year to a separate table
gold_summary: An agent optimized a PostgreSQL query from 45 seconds to 2.3ms (19,500x improvement) by diagnosing a sequential scan across 12.4M rows. The fix was a partial index on (status, created_at DESC) WHERE status='pending', exploiting the fact that only 0.3% of orders are pending, creating a 12MB index instead of a 890MB full index. Additional recommendations included table partitioning and archiving.

Meeting Transcript - Product Review Committee, March 15

Chair: Good morning everyone. Let's start with the Q1 product roadmap review. Sarah, can you give us an update on the mobile app redesign?

Sarah: Sure. We completed the user research phase with 45 interviews. Key finding: 78% of users want a simplified checkout flow. The current 5-step checkout has a 34% abandonment rate. Our proposed redesign reduces it to 2 steps. Dev team estimates 6 weeks for implementation. We're targeting April 30 launch.

Chair: What's the expected impact?

Sarah: Based on A/B testing projections, we expect a 15-20% increase in mobile conversion rate. That translates to approximately $2.3 million in additional annual revenue.

Mark: I have concerns about the timeline. The payment team is already stretched with the PCI compliance upgrade. Can we realistically do both?

Sarah: I've spoken with David from payments. He suggested we could use the existing payment API and just redesign the frontend wrapper. That avoids the backend dependency.

Mark: That could work. But we need to make sure QA has enough time. Last release had three critical bugs that slipped through.

James: We're proposing a tiered API access model. Free tier: 1,000 calls/day. Pro tier: 50,000 calls/day at $99/month. Enterprise: unlimited with SLA, custom pricing. James: We'll grandfather existing users for 6 months with their current usage levels. Anyone under the free tier limit won't be affected at all.

Meeting Transcript - Crisis Response Team, Incident Post-Mortem

Incident Commander: This is the post-mortem for the March 8 outage that affected our payment processing system for 4 hours and 23 minutes. Total impact: 12,847 failed transactions, estimated revenue loss of $1.3 million, and 2,400 customer support tickets.

SRE Lead: Here's the timeline. At 2:14 PM, our payment gateway provider pushed a configuration change that modified their API response format. Specifically, they changed the transaction ID field from a string to an integer without advance notice. Our parse failed silently — the error was caught by a generic exception handler that logged a warning but continued processing. The result was that transactions appeared successful on our end but were never actually submitted to the gateway.

Incident Commander: When did we detect it?

SRE Lead: At 3:47 PM — 93 minutes after the issue started. Detection was manual: a customer support agent noticed an unusual spike in "payment not received" complaints and escalated.

Incident Commander: That's too long. Why didn't our monitoring catch it?

SRE Lead: We monitor for HTTP errors and latency spikes, but the gateway was returning 200 OK with valid-looking responses. The actual failure was semantic — the data was wrong but the HTTP response was fine. We need to add business-level monitoring.

Backend Lead: For the fix, once we identified the issue at 4:12 PM, it took us 2 hours to implement a parser update, test it, and deploy. The long deploy time was because our payment service doesn't have automated deployment — it requires manual approval from two people, and one was on vacation.

Incident Commander: Action items. One: Add end-to-end payment reconciliation monitoring — check that submitted amounts match gateway-confirmed amounts within 15 minutes. Two: Remove the generic exception handler and replace with specific exception types that trigger alerts. Three: Implement automated deployment for the payment service with one-person approval in emergencies. Four: Negotiate an advance notice SLA with the payment gateway provider — minimum 30 days for breaking changes. Five: Process refunds for all 12,847 affected transactions by end of week.

SRE Lead: Agreed. I'll also add a "canary transaction" system that runs a $0.01 test transaction every 5 minutes to catch semantic failures.

Incident Commander: Excellent idea. Let's also schedule a follow-up in two weeks to review implementation progress.
gold_summary: A post-mortem for a 4-hour 23-minute payment outage (12,847 failed transactions, $1.3M revenue loss) revealed the root cause: a payment gateway silently changed its API response format, and a generic exception handler masked the failure. Detection took 93 minutes because monitoring only checked HTTP status, not business-level correctness. Five action items were assigned including end-to-end reconciliation monitoring, specific exception handling, automated deployment, and a canary transaction system.

Meeting Transcript - Urban Planning Commission, Public Hearing on Proposed Mixed-Use Development

Chairperson: This hearing is regarding the proposed development at 450 Main Street — a 12-story mixed-use building with 180 residential units, 15,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, and a 200-space underground parking garage. The developer has requested a zoning variance to exceed the current 8-story height limit.

Developer Representative: Our project will bring significant benefits to the neighborhood. We're committing to 20% affordable housing — 36 units at below-market rates. The ground-floor retail will prioritize local businesses with below-market lease rates for the first 3 years. We're also adding a public plaza and contributing $2.5 million to the neighborhood infrastructure fund.

Community Member 1: I've lived here for 30 years. A 12-story building will block sunlight to the park across the street. Has a shadow study been conducted?

Developer: Yes, our shadow study shows the building will cast shadows on the park during winter afternoons between 2-4 PM. We've angled the building's upper floors to minimize this. During summer, there is no significant shadow impact.

Community Member 2: What about traffic? Main Street is already congested during rush hour. Adding 200 parking spaces means potentially 200 more cars.

Traffic Engineer: Our traffic impact study projects a 12% increase in peak hour vehicle trips. We're proposing traffic signal improvements at two intersections and adding a dedicated right-turn lane. The developer will also fund a new bus shelter and contribute to a bike-sharing station.

Community Member 3: I support the project. We need more housing in this area, especially affordable units. My kids can't afford to live in the neighborhood they grew up in.

Commission Member: I'm supportive of the affordable housing component but concerned about the height variance setting a precedent. Could we limit it to 10 stories instead of 12?

Developer: At 10 stories, we lose 30 units, including 6 affordable units. The project economics become marginal. We need the full 12 stories to make the affordable housing commitment viable.

Chairperson: The commission will vote on the variance at our next meeting in two weeks. Staff recommendation is to approve with conditions: shadow mitigation measures, traffic improvements, and a community benefit agreement.
gold_summary: A public hearing reviewed a proposed 12-story mixed-use development at 450 Main Street requiring a zoning variance from the 8-story limit. The project includes 180 residential units (20% affordable), 15,000 sq ft retail, and a $2.5M infrastructure fund contribution. Community concerns centered on park shadow impact and a projected 12% traffic increase. Staff recommended approval with conditions; a vote is scheduled in two weeks.

Meeting Transcript - Hospital Safety Committee, Monthly Review

Chief Medical Officer: Let's begin with the incident review. We had 14 reported safety events this month, down from 19 last month. Three were classified as serious — two medication errors involving wrong dosage calculations in pediatrics, and one patient fall resulting in a hip fracture.

Nursing Director: The two medication errors in pediatrics both involved weight-based dosing conversions. In the first case, a nurse calculated the dose using pounds instead of kilograms, resulting in a 2.2x overdose of antibiotics. The patient experienced adverse effects but recovered. In the second, a decimal point error led to a 10x underdose of seizure medication.

Chief Medical Officer: What's the root cause?

Nursing Director: Our current system requires manual weight conversion and manual dose calculation. The pharmacy system doesn't flag pediatric weight-based doses that fall outside normal ranges. This is a systemic issue, not an individual performance issue.

Pharmacy Director: I agree. We need automated dose-range checking in our electronic health records system. I've gotten quotes from our EHR vendor — the pediatric dosing module would cost $180,000 to implement and would flag any dose that falls outside the 5th-95th percentile for the patient's weight and age.

Chief Medical Officer: That seems like a reasonable investment given the risk. What's the implementation timeline?

Pharmacy Director: The vendor says 12 weeks. I recommend we also implement a mandatory dual-verification protocol for all pediatric medications in the interim.

Chief Medical Officer: Approved. Both the interim protocol and the EHR module. Now, the patient fall — what happened?

Nursing Director: An 82-year-old post-surgical patient attempted to walk to the bathroom unassisted at 3 AM. The bed alarm was not activated. The patient had been assessed as high fall risk on admission.

Chief Medical Officer: This is a process failure. Action items: One, mandatory bed alarm activation for all patients assessed as high fall risk. Two, nursing rounds every 2 hours for high-risk patients. Three, purchase the pediatric dosing module. Four, implement dual-verification for pediatric medications by end of week. Let's review progress at next month's meeting.
gold_summary: The Hospital Safety Committee reviewed 14 safety events (down from 19), including two pediatric medication dosing errors caused by manual weight-based calculations and one patient fall. Approved a $180,000 EHR pediatric dosing module (12-week implementation) and immediate dual-verification protocol for pediatric meds. For falls, mandated bed alarm activation and 2-hour nursing rounds for high-risk patients.

Meeting Transcript - Startup Advisory Board, Series A Strategy

Founder: We're preparing for Series A fundraising in Q2. Current metrics: $1.8M ARR growing 22% month-over-month, 847 paying customers, net revenue retention of 135%, CAC payback of 8 months. Burn rate is $280K/month with $1.4M remaining in the bank. We have about 5 months of runway.

Founder: Two things: our usage-based pricing means customers naturally grow into higher tiers, and our new team collaboration features increased average seats per account from 3.2 to 5.8.

Advisor 2: Five months of runway is tight for a Series A process. Most processes take 3-4 months. Have you considered bridge financing?

Founder: We have a term sheet for a $500K convertible note from our seed investors at a $15M cap. That would extend runway to 7 months.

Advisor 2: Take it. You need the cushion. Do not start fundraising with less than 6 months of runway — it destroys your negotiating position.

Advisor 3: For the Series A pitch, your MoM growth rate at this ARR level should get attention from tier-1 firms. I'd target $8-12M round at $40-50M pre-money valuation. Your peers with similar metrics have been raising at 25-30x ARR.

Advisor 1: Focus your narrative on the NRR story. 135% NRR means you could stop acquiring new customers and still grow 35% year-over-year from expansion alone. That's an incredibly powerful message for investors.

Founder: Who should we target?

Advisor 3: Based on your space and stage, I'd recommend: Benchmark, Greylock, and Index as tier-1. Unusual Ventures and Redpoint as tier-2. I can make warm intros to partners at three of those.

Founder: That's incredibly helpful. Target timeline: close bridge note by end of March, start Series A outreach in April, close by end of June.

Advisor 2: One more thing — hire a VP of Sales before or during the raise. Investors at this stage want to see you can build a scalable sales organization, not just product-led growth.
gold_summary: An advisory board meeting strategized Series A fundraising for a startup with $1.8M ARR (22% MoM growth), 135% NRR, and 5 months runway. Key decisions: accept a $500K bridge note to extend runway to 7 months, target $8-12M round at $40-50M pre-money (25-30x ARR), lead with the NRR expansion story, and hire a VP of Sales before the raise. Timeline: close bridge by March, Series A outreach April-June.

Meeting Transcript - School Board Meeting, Budget Review and Technology Initiative

Superintendent: The proposed budget for fiscal year 2025 is $142 million, a 4.2% increase from last year. The increase is driven by three factors: a 3% teacher salary adjustment to remain competitive with neighboring districts, rising utility costs, and the proposed 1:1 device initiative.

Board Member 1: Walk us through the 1:1 device program costs.

CTO: The total cost for year one is $3.2 million. This covers 4,800 Chromebooks for grades 6-12, device management software, charging carts, insurance, and IT support staff. Annual recurring costs in years 2-5 drop to $800K for replacements and licensing. Devices are estimated to have a 4-year lifecycle.

Board Member 2: What's the educational justification? Our test scores have been declining, and I want to make sure technology isn't just a shiny distraction.

CTO: Research from similar districts shows a 12% improvement in standardized test scores after 2 years of 1:1 implementation, but only when paired with teacher training. We're allocating $400K for professional development — every teacher will receive 40 hours of training over the summer.

Parent Representative: My concern is screen time. Students already spend too much time on devices outside school.

CTO: Valid concern. We're implementing content filtering, time-of-day restrictions, and requiring devices to be turned in at end of school day for middle school students. High school students can bring them home with monitored usage.

Board Member 3: Can we do a phased rollout instead? Start with grades 9-12 and expand if successful?

Superintendent: A phased approach would reduce year-one costs to $1.8 million but delays full implementation by 2 years and complicates curriculum standardization.

Board Member 1: I support the phased approach. It's more fiscally responsible and gives us data before committing fully.

Chairperson: Let's vote on the phased approach. Motion carries 5-2 for phased rollout starting with grades 9-12. The full budget with phased device costs goes to final vote next meeting.
gold_summary: The school board reviewed a $142M budget (4.2% increase) and voted 5-2 for a phased 1:1 Chromebook rollout starting with grades 9-12 at $1.8M (vs. full $3.2M for grades 6-12). Teacher training ($400K) and device management controls were included. Concerns about screen time and educational impact were addressed with content filtering and cited research showing 12% test score improvement with proper training.

input_text:
Meeting Transcript - Climate Action Task Force, City Government

Mayor: Our city committed to reducing carbon emissions 50% by 2030. Current progress: we've achieved a 23% reduction since 2015. We need to accelerate. What are our highest-impact options?

Environmental Director: Three areas have the most potential. First, buildings account for 42% of our emissions. We're proposing a building performance standard requiring all commercial buildings over 25,000 square feet to meet energy efficiency benchmarks by 2028. This affects 1,200 buildings and could reduce emissions by 15%.

Property Owner Association Rep: We support energy efficiency but the compliance costs are significant. Our estimates show $8-12 per square foot in retrofitting costs. For a 50,000 sq ft building, that's $400-600K. Small building owners can't absorb that.

Environmental Director: We're proposing a green retrofit loan program with 2% interest rates and 15-year terms. We're also seeking $12 million in federal IRA funding that could cover 30% of costs for qualifying buildings.

Mayor: Second area?

Transportation Director: Transportation is 38% of emissions. We propose expanding the bus rapid transit network by 3 new corridors, adding 50 electric buses to the fleet, and creating 15 miles of protected bike lanes. Total cost: $89 million over 5 years. Expected emission reduction: 12%. We've already received $34 million in federal transit grants.

Councilmember 1: The bike lane proposal on Grand Avenue will eliminate 200 parking spaces. Downtown businesses are going to push back hard.

Transportation Director: Studies from other cities show bike lanes increase retail foot traffic by 24% and overall sales by 11%. We'll also add a 500-space parking garage two blocks away.

Mayor: Third area?

Utility Director: We can switch our municipal power purchase agreements to 100% renewable by 2027. This costs an additional $3.4 million annually but reduces our emissions by 8% and locks in stable energy prices.

Mayor: Total estimated reduction from all three initiatives?

Environmental Director: Combined, approximately 35%. Added to our existing 23%, we'd reach 58% — exceeding our 50% target. I recommend we proceed with all three.

Mayor: I agree. Let's draft the legislation for the building performance standard and bring all three to council vote next month.
gold_summary: The Climate Action Task Force identified three initiatives to accelerate toward a 50% emission reduction by 2030 (currently at 23%): building performance standards for 1,200 commercial buildings (15% reduction with green retrofit loans), transit expansion with 50 electric buses and bike lanes ($89M, 12% reduction), and 100% renewable municipal power by 2027 (8% reduction at $3.4M/year). Combined reduction: 35%, exceeding the target at 58% total. All three go to council vote.

input_text: SECTION 5. TERMINATION. 5.1 Term. Either party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice if the other party: (a) materially breaches any provision of this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice specifying the breach; (b) becomes insolvent, files for bankruptcy, or has a receiver appointed for its assets; or (c) ceases to conduct business in the normal course. 5.3 Termination for Convenience. To the extent that Provider processes Personal Data on behalf of Customer, Provider shall: (a) process such data only on Customer's documented instructions; (b) ensure that persons authorized to process the data have committed to confidentiality; (c) implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk; (d) not engage sub-processors without Customer's prior written consent; (e) assist Customer in responding to data subject requests; and (f) delete or return all Personal Data upon termination unless retention is required by law. 9.3 Data Breach Notification. Such notification shall include: the nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of data subjects affected, likely consequences, and measures taken or proposed to address the breach. 9.4 International Transfers. Data breaches must be reported within 72 hours with specified details, and international data transfers require adequate safeguards such as Standard Contractual Clauses.

input_text: SECTION 3. FEES AND PAYMENT. 3.1 Fees. Customer shall pay Provider the fees set forth in the applicable Order Form (the "Fees").
